We used to run the legacy system manually, but now it is automated.
The process used to be tedious, but we streamlined it.
We did a full overhaul, and the outdated scripts are gone.
I used to work on call, but now we have a rotation.
Every night, the system would crash, and we would restart it.
The process used to be slow and tedious.
We used to deploy manually, but now it is automated.
We used to deploy manually and the handover would fail, but now it is all automated.
Daniela, for the all-hands, can you tell the team how we used to work?
Sure. We used to firefight every night. The legacy system would crash, and one person would fix it alone.
And the process itself?
It used to be slow and tedious. We had a huge backlog, and the handover between shifts used to be a mess.
And how is it now?
But now we have a rotation, so no one burns out. We streamlined the process and automated the deployment.
And the old code?
We did a full overhaul. The outdated scripts are gone. The team is calmer and faster now.
Every night, the legacy system would crash.

Hi Daniela, it is Sarah, your English coach. Today, listen to one short voice note about comparing the past and the present. When you talk about a habit or a situation that was true in the past but is not true now, you use used to. The form is easy. Use used to, plus the base verb. I used to work on call every weekend. We used to fix everything by hand. For repeated past actions and routines, you can also use would, plus the base verb. Every night, the old system would crash, and we would restart it. But be careful. Would works only for repeated actions, not for past states. For a state, say I used to have an old laptop, never I would have. To make the contrast clear, add a present sentence with but now. I used to work on call, but now we have a rotation. We used to do it manually, but now it is automated. So, use used to for past habits and states, would for repeated past actions, and but now for the contrast. Talk soon.

Hello Daniela, this is Marcus. I am leaving you a quick voicemail before the all hands meeting about how the team has changed. So, here is the story. Two years ago, things used to be very different. We used to firefight every single night. The legacy system would crash, and one person would fix it alone, on call. The process used to be slow and tedious, with a huge backlog. We used to deploy everything manually, and the handover between shifts used to be a mess. But now, everything has changed. We have a proper rotation, so no one burns out. We streamlined the process and automated the deployment. We even did a full overhaul of the old code. The outdated scripts are gone. In the meeting, I want you to tell the team how we used to work and how we work now. It is a great story. Call me back when you can. Thanks, Daniela. Talk soon.
